text.title: Dependency-Graph Reasoning Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that turn a program dependency graph from a one-off Mermaid diagram into a queryable, calibrated asset.

rule.statement: Every edge MUST be typed: FS (finish-to-start), SS (start-to-start), FF (finish-to-finish), SF (start-to-finish), OR cross-team HANDOFF with a named artifact (API spec, design pack, data feed). Untyped "depends on" edges are rejected — they conflate "must finish before" with "must coordinate with."
rule.rationale: Different edge types produce different at-risk analyses. FS slip propagates; SS slip may not. Without types the graph cannot answer the queries.

rule.statement: The graph MUST live in a single committed file: Mermaid + Markdown, DOT + Graphviz, or JSON. Tool-of-record diagrams in Miro / Lucidchart are NOT the source of truth — at most they are renderings. Diffs, blame, and PR review apply to the file.
rule.rationale: Tool-bound diagrams die when the tool subscription lapses or the editor leaves. Code-form graphs survive personnel and tool turnover and integrate with CI.

rule.statement: The graph MUST be refreshed at a fixed cadence (default weekly) from team trackers. The refresh produces a diff (new nodes / removed nodes / changed edges) and the diff is reviewed before merge. Graphs older than 14 days are flagged stale and excluded from risk reports.

rationale.source: Empirical: program graphs drift faster than expected; even monthly refresh misses 30%+ of new dependencies

rule.rationale: Weekly is the minimum cadence that keeps the graph calibrated. The diff is what catches teams adding hidden dependencies.

rule.statement: The PM MUST run at least three standard queries per week: (a) blocked-by-status — what is currently blocked, (b) at-risk-if-slip-1-week — propagation analysis for the top-3 at-risk nodes, (c) longest-path-to-milestone — critical path for each upcoming cross-team milestone. Ad-hoc queries are allowed but the three are mandatory.

rationale.source: Critical-path method + Theory of Constraints applied to multi-team programs

rule.rationale: PMs default to looking only at currently-blocked items. The standard query set surfaces near-future blockers and structural risks before they become incidents.

rule.statement: Changes to the graph MUST come via PR-style change requests with author + reason. Direct edits without a PR (or equivalent change record) are forbidden. The PR review surfaces hidden assumptions ("why is Team B suddenly dependent on Team C's API?").

rationale.source: Code-review discipline applied to program artifacts

rule.rationale: Silent edits erode trust. PR review makes dependency claims defensible and surfaces the "I didn't know we needed that" moments early.

example.title: Typed-edge graph fragment
example.code: %% Mermaid program graph graph LR TA_api_v2[Team A: API v2 GA] -- FS --> TB_billing[Team B: Billing on v2] TA_api_v2 -- HANDOFF(openapi.yaml) --> TC_sdk[Team C: SDK regeneration] TB_billing -- FS --> M_q3_launch[Milestone: Q3 launch] TC_sdk -- FS --> M_q3_launch
example.note: Team-prefixed nodes, typed edges with handoff artifact, single milestone target. Queryable.

example.title: Untyped soup
example.code: api_v2 --> billing api_v2 --> sdk billing --> launch sdk --> launch "these are all kind of blocking"
example.note: No team prefixes, no edge types, no handoff artifacts. Cannot answer the "what if API v2 slips" question.
